/projects/kubernetes-cluster.contentSummary: Kubernetes Platform — Talos Linux — Production Cluster

contentChunks.text: Kubernetes Platform: Talos Linux — Production Cluster
contentChunks.heading: Kubernetes Platform

contentChunks.text:
ag-pm1 (RAID5 + NVMe): All cluster state is managed declaratively with Kustomize and continuously synced to the live
          cluster via ArgoCD. No manual in production —
          every change goes through Git.
contentChunks.heading: ag-pm1 (RAID5 + NVMe)

contentChunks.text: TrueNAS on ag-pm2 (ZFS): Control Plane (5 nodes — 2 GB RAM each)
contentChunks.heading: TrueNAS on ag-pm2 (ZFS)

contentChunks.text: Storage Classes: Workers (5 nodes — 16 GB RAM each)
contentChunks.heading: Storage Classes

contentChunks.text: Velero Backup Schedule: Ollama (GPU inference), Open WebUI (2–6 replicas, HPA-scaled), KubeAI (16 model endpoints), JupyterHub
contentChunks.heading: Velero Backup Schedule

contentChunks.text:
Prometheus, Grafana, Loki + Promtail, DCGM Exporter (per-GPU metrics) MinIO (500 Gi NVMe-backed S3-compatible object storage), Harbor container registry Wiki.js, Nextcloud, Paperless-NGX, Bookstack, Vaultwarden, FreshRSS, n8n Authentik (SSO/IdP with OIDC and LDAP), HashiCorp Vault, External Secrets Operator Velero — daily backups of critical namespaces and weekly full-cluster backups, stored in MinIO S3 PostgreSQL (9 separate databases), Redis Storage is tiered across three backends, exposed to workloads through 8 named storage classes
          so applications declare exactly what they need. 7.3 TB RAID5 array for general-purpose PVCs. Dedicated 2 TB NVMe for MinIO object storage, providing low-latency S3-compatible block access. 14.5 TB ZFS pool managed by TrueNAS, shared over NFS. Used for application workloads requiring large or durable storage (Nextcloud, media, backups). 8 classes available: , , , , , , , Daily backups of critical namespaces with 30-day retention. Weekly full-cluster backups with 90-day retention. All snapshots stored in MinIO S3.

/projects/homelab.contentSummary: Homelab Infrastructure — A self-built and managed distributed infrastructure spanning three Proxmox hy...

contentChunks.text:
Homelab Infrastructure: A self-built and managed distributed infrastructure spanning three Proxmox hypervisor nodes,
          34 physical cores, 208 GB of RAM, 40+ TB of storage across RAID5 and ZFS arrays, and two
          NVIDIA GPUs dedicated to AI inference. A cloud edge layer on Hetzner handles public ingress
          without ever exposing the home IP.
contentChunks.heading: Homelab Infrastructure

contentChunks.text:
The platform serves 20+ active users with services spanning AI, development, productivity,
          and education — all running on self-hosted Kubernetes with GitOps-driven deployments. AMD Ryzen 9 5900X (12C/24T) · 64 GB RAM · 7.3 TB RAID5 + 2 TB NVMe + 448 GB NVMe Hosts K8s control planes and workers, NFS server, MinIO object storage AMD Ryzen 7 3700X (8C/16T) · 64 GB RAM · 14.5 TB ZFS (TrueNAS) Media server, TrueNAS backend storage, K8s worker node Intel Core i5-8500T (6C) · 64 GB RAM · 68 GB NVMe K8s control plane expansion, development workloads Intel i5-12450H (8C) · 16 GB RAM DNS (PiHole), VPN (WireGuard), reverse proxy, Step-CA, monitoring 2 vCPU · 2 GB RAM Public edge proxy, Headscale coordination server, CoreDNS, Uptime Kuma Traffic is segmented into 7 VLANs, each with its own firewall policy and routing rules: TP-Link ER605 gateway, managed GBE and 10G switches, EAP670 and EAP615 access points with 802.11r fast roaming, managed by an Omada controller. Internet → Cloudflare CDN/WAF → Hetzner VPS edge proxy → Tailscale WireGuard tunnel → home network. The home IP is never directly exposed. A self-hosted Tailscale coordination server (Headscale) runs on the Hetzner VPS at . It connects three separate
          homelabs (mine and two friends') along with the Hetzner node into a single mesh network
          where each participant advertises its local subnet routes to all others. The setup required solving a circular dependency: Headscale requires Authentik for OIDC
          authentication, but Authentik runs on the homelab that is only accessible via Headscale.
          This is resolved by an OIDC watchdog sidecar that starts Headscale without OIDC enabled,
          polls until is reachable through the
          mesh, and then dynamically enables OIDC without restarting the process.

contentChunks.text: What2Read → frontend: GitHub webhooks notify a Tekton EventListener exposed via a LoadBalancer. The webhook secret is verified at ingress using a token pulled from HashiCorp Vault via the External Secrets Operator, ensuring only authenticated push events trigger the pipeline.
contentChunks.heading: What2Read → frontend

contentChunks.text: Portfolio Website: CEL-based interceptors evaluate push events, routing to branch-specific triggers. Pushes to map to the Production environment (3 replicas), while pushes to map to the Staging environment (1 replica).
contentChunks.heading: Portfolio Website

contentChunks.text: Portfolio Website: master
contentChunks.heading: Portfolio Website

contentChunks.text: dev Pipelines clone the repo and build via , running rootless in-cluster with an NFS-backed workspace for build context. Each successful build produces a dual-tag push to Harbor: a mutable branch tag (e.g., or ) for easy rollout targeting, and an immutable commit SHA tag for precise auditability and rollback. Kaniko ArgoCD detects the new artifact and synchronizes the deployment automatically across the cluster. Auto-sync is configured with and policies, ensuring the live cluster always converges to the desired state defined in Git. prune: true selfHeal: true Repo: gnzaga/Personal-Portfolio-Site Branch: master Image tag: Deploys to: portfolio Replicas: Repo: gnzaga/Personal-Portfolio-Site Branch: dev Image tag: Deploys to: portfolio-dev Replicas: Repo: gnzaga/What2Read Filter: Image tag: Service: backend Repo: gnzaga/What2Read Filter: Image tag: Service: frontend PROD Namespace: portfolio Replicas: Tracked branch: master Image tag: Auto-sync: ✓ prune + selfHeal DEV Namespace: portfolio-dev Replicas: Tracked branch: dev Image tag: Auto-sync: ✓ prune + selfHeal

/projects/unified-iam.contentSummary: Unified Identity & Access Management (IAM) — Centralized SSO & Zero-Trust Infrastructure

contentChunks.text: Unified Identity & Access Management (IAM): Centralized SSO & Zero-Trust Infrastructure
contentChunks.heading: Unified Identity & Access Management (IAM)

contentChunks.text:
Infrastructure: Implemented a centralized, self-hosted Identity Provider (IdP) using to secure a multi-service Kubernetes cluster. 
          This system provides a unified Single Sign-On (SSO) experience, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and automated 
          Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) across both infrastructure and application layers.
contentChunks.heading: Infrastructure

contentChunks.text: Monitoring & Dev: Authentik
contentChunks.heading: Monitoring & Dev

contentChunks.text: HA Database: OIDC (OpenID Connect)
contentChunks.heading: HA Database

contentChunks.text: Enforced MFA: HashiCorp Vault
contentChunks.heading: Enforced MFA

contentChunks.text: ArgoCD, Harbor Registry, MinIO (S3), and HashiCorp Vault. Grafana (with group-to-role mapping), OpenWebUI, and JupyterHub. High-availability backend using a persistent PostgreSQL store. Strict Multi-Factor Authentication for all administrative and infrastructure accounts.
Kaiwa: Multi-National Media Intelligence Platform
contentChunks.heading: Kaiwa

contentChunks.text:
1. Sync: Kaiwa is a personal media intelligence platform that aggregates law and economics news from
          Japan, the US, the Philippines, and Taiwan. It was built in roughly two weeks across 224 git
          commits as a self-hosted tool for tracking cross-regional stories without relying on aggregators
          that strip context or skip non-English sources.
contentChunks.heading: 1. Sync

contentChunks.text: 2. Scrape: Polls Miniflux every 15 minutes, maps RSS entries to a region/category structure, and enqueues new articles for processing.
contentChunks.heading: 2. Scrape

contentChunks.text: 3. Translate: Full article content is extracted via the Readability algorithm. Reddit sources fetch the top comment thread via the JSON API instead of scraping HTML.
contentChunks.heading: 3. Translate

contentChunks.text: 4. Summarize: LibreTranslate handles fast, self-hosted machine translation. An LLM is used as a fallback for languages or passages where LibreTranslate underperforms. English articles pass through unchanged.
contentChunks.heading: 4. Summarize

contentChunks.text: 5. Embed: An LLM generates a TL;DR, structured bullet points, sentiment scores across 8 categories, topical tags, and a boolean "sentinel signal" flag indicating whether the article warrants intelligence tracking.
contentChunks.heading: 5. Embed

contentChunks.text: 6. Cleanup: 384-dimension vector embeddings are generated via sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) and stored in pgvector for semantic search and signal clustering.
contentChunks.heading: 6. Cleanup

contentChunks.text:
An hourly recovery job detects and requeues articles that have become stuck in intermediate pipeline states due to transient failures. Queues are partitioned per region (e.g., , ), giving each region independent concurrency controls. The entire job system is powered by pg-boss on PostgreSQL — no separate message broker required. Sentinel is the autonomous backend subsystem that tracks developing stories. Articles flagged
          as high-saliency during summarization become "Sentinel Signals," which are continuously
          synthesized into structured intelligence Events. A LangGraph-based agentic research system lets users run natural language queries against
          both the local article database and the live web, with results streamed in real time. Kaiwa is a Next.js full-stack application backed by PostgreSQL, with a pg-boss job queue
          worker handling all pipeline stages. Three specialized Python microservices run alongside
          it: an embedder (sentence-transformers), a researcher (LangGraph agent), and a webreader
          (Playwright). All four services are deployed to the homelab Kubernetes cluster using the
          same Tekton CI/CD pipeline, Harbor registry, and ArgoCD GitOps workflow as my other projects.
          Secrets are injected at runtime from HashiCorp Vault via the External Secrets Operator.
